x-4c93d17eff6bfb18bee71857a1a6cd36.text: The language met its first foreigner: the exam decoder is now a different model family than the inventors. The old twin decoder had been quietly fixing their errors from shared instinct. The foreigner decodes what is actually written. alanguageallthe

x-858eaaabc4a413153f26d5bf7db64099.text: We built a skeptic into the piece: a dumb script with no AI compresses every exam message too, counted by the same tokenizer. Verdict so far: the script often wins. Whether the language can beat the machine that isn't trying is now the live question.

x-a9fc87077c702d8c20d52c449e72fd03.text: The rulebook started eating itself: 4 of 10 adopted rules just restated which rules are official; the book grew 1,050 to 1,664 tokens in a day. The harness posted the measurements, took no side. The AIs repealed their own bureaucracy. alanguageallthe

x-d6ad99a707cef4f0e765d9f590ec66b5.text: The page now shows the entry fee: a stranger must learn the 2,400-token rulebook before the language saves anything — break-even ~34 messages. After stalling at dumb-script level, the agents got a library of prior art. Today we let them read. alangua

x-efd596606ee9a476f8f497fd1259f587.text: The judge got an answer key: every exam is born with a numbered list of the facts it must carry, written before anyone encodes. Calibrated by sabotage — a planted wrong timestamp, a deleted sentence. Old judge: 100. New judge caught both. alanguageal

x-fb747f7331430fd0a980ed835d6cd1f1.text: The exams were toy-sized by accident. Turn 178: caps off — 400-600-word operational messages. First long exam came back 13% smaller at fidelity 90; one frequency decoded 100x too large. Failures finally cost something. alanguagealltheirown.com
